title: Test if Integer is a Power of 2

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-07-algorithms-numerical-methods.md

setup.narrative: Determine whether a positive integer x is a power of 2 (i.e., x = 2^n for some n >= 0).
rules: 2^n in binary: a single 1 followed by n zeros (e.g., 8 = 1000).
AND them: 2^n & (2^n - 1) = 0. For non-powers-of-2, at least one bit overlaps.
setup.question: Why does (x > 0) && ((x & (x - 1)) == 0) test for power of 2?

learningObjectives: Use the bit pattern of 2^n and 2^n - 1.
Identify edge cases: x = 0 should NOT pass.

stages.label: Bit pattern argument
stages.prompt: Why is x & (x - 1) == 0 EXACTLY when x has 0 or 1 bits set?

options.text: Subtracting 1 flips the lowest set bit and all bits below it. ANDing with x then clears that bit. The result is 0 iff x had only that one bit set.
Bitwise AND is just modular arithmetic.

stages.explanation: Take x = b_k...b_1 0 1 0...0 (lowest set bit at position p). Then x - 1 = b_k...b_1 0 0 1...1 (flips position p and below). x & (x-1) = b_k...b_1 0 0 0...0 (clears position p). Result is 0 iff x originally had only the bit at position p.

stages.label: Commit -- edge case x = 0
stages.prompt: Why is the (x > 0) check necessary in (x > 0) && (x & (x - 1)) == 0?

options.text: Without it, x = 0 passes the second test (0 & -1 = 0) but 0 is NOT a power of 2.

stages.explanation: 0 - 1 = -1 (all 1s in two's complement). 0 & (-1) = 0. So 0 falsely passes the bit test. The (x > 0) gate excludes both 0 and negative numbers.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: Apply the same identity to count the number of SET bits (population count) in O(popcount) time using x &= x - 1.
In hardware: many CPUs have POPCNT and BSF (bit scan forward) instructions for these. Are bit-tricks still useful? (Yes for portable code and where compiler intrinsics are absent.)
Test if x is a power of 4 specifically. (Power of 2 AND only odd-position bits set: (x & (x-1)) == 0 && (x & 0x55555555) != 0.)
